text: BHP half-year results: underlying EBITDA of US$13.9bn, net debt within target range. Management flags iron ore price sensitivity and notes exposure to interest rate movements on the balance sheet refinancing due next year.

RBA statement: the cash rate target was held at 4.35 percent. The Board judges inflation is returning to the 2-3 percent band but remains alert to upside risks; the path of interest rates will depend on incoming data.

Market news: BHP announced a further US$500m investment in its Pilbara iron ore automation program, expecting unit cost reductions from FY27. Analysts view the capital allocation as defensive against a softening ore price outlook.

Product disclosure statement excerpt: the fund's diversified strategy allocates 40 percent to Australian equities with a materials-sector overweight, noting concentration risk when single-name holdings exceed 8 percent of portfolio value.

Modern Award MA000004 (General Retail Industry Award): casual employees are entitled to a 25 percent loading on the ordinary hourly rate, with penalty rates for work after 6pm and on weekends as set out in clause 23.

Superannuation guarantee ruling: employers must contribute 12 percent of ordinary time earnings from 1 July 2025. Failure to remit by the quarterly deadline triggers the superannuation guarantee charge including interest and administration components.

Caselaw summary: in a 2024 Fair Work Commission decision, an employee misclassified as a casual over four years was found entitled to backdated annual leave; the Commission applied the substance-over-form test to the employment relationship.

Small lift on slow trading. The Australian sharemarket has finished the second trading day of the year up slightly, as thin volumes – thanks to many traders enjoying holidays – leave the bourse with a lack of conviction to move. The Australian sharemarket has finished the second trading day of the year up slightly, as thin volumes – thanks to many traders enjoying holidays – leave the bourse with a lack of conviction to move.

The benchmark S&P/ASX 200 Index added 14.4 points, or 0.3 per cent, to 5450.3, while the broader All Ordinaries Index gained 14.5 points, or 0.3 per cent to 5429.5.

"The volumes aren't there, the bid offers are pretty light. We saw the market open negatively, which was a little bit of a surprise because Wall Street did pretty much nothing on ­Friday," said Quay Equities head of trading Tristan

PORTRAIT. The architect with a soft spot for public buildings was in his element working on Australia’s sentimental favourite, writes Pip Coates. The architect with a soft spot for public buildings was in his element working on Australia’s sentimental favourite, writes Pip Coates.

When tasked with redesigning the Sydney Opera House’s flagship restaurant space, the temptation could be to try to leave a mark.

But Sydney architect Tim Greer reckons there’s no room – nor public appetite – for ego where such a significant and much revered building is concerned.

“Everybody – except my late aunt – loves the Opera House,” laughs Greer, who first set eyes on the Jørn Utzon-designed building in 1986, two years before he moved to Australia from

Business blasts IR blueprint. Business has blasted the Productivity Commission's proposed blueprint for the nation's workplace system as an "opportunity lost", with BHP Billiton leading the charge to urge Malcolm Turnbull to seek a mandate for more ambitious policy changes at next year's federal election. Business has blasted the Productivity Commission's proposed blueprint for the nation's workplace system as an "opportunity lost", with BHP Billiton leading the charge to urge Malcolm Turnbull to seek a mandate for more ambitious policy changes at next year's federal election.

But as unions promised a multimillion-dollar marginal seats campaign on workplace relations, the Coalition refused to commit to supporting any of the recommendation's in the commission's final report into the workplace relations framework, saying it will consult further before considering whether to seek voter backing fo

Brandis 'not at liberty' on new role.  Attorney-General George Brandis says he is "not at liberty" to say whether he is taking up a diplomatic or judicial role in the near future and denied that conflict over a High Court case was the cause of a breakdown of his relationship with former solicitor-general Justin Gleeson.

Freshly returned from a visit to London and Paris, Senator Brandis was asked about his plans by a Senate committee.

There has been considerable speculation that Senator Brandis will replace Alexander Downer as High Commissioner to London next year, after a series of mishaps have left him as a liability within the

metadata.headline: Brandis 'not at liberty' on new role

text:
Downer closing in on target in bid for Spotless.  Downer EDI has secured 33 per cent of Spotless as the contractor's hostile $1.2 billion takeover bid for the services group picks up steam ahead of the bid's closure at the end of the month.

But the chief executive of Spotless, Martin Sheppard, said on Friday morning that he was just getting on with normal day-to-day operations and not being distracted as the bid process headed to a crucial point.

"We are running the business as if we didn't have the distraction of Downer at all," Mr Sheppard said.

The mood inside Spotless at its Melbourne headquarters was still upbeat and people were going

metadata.headline: Downer closing in on target in bid for Spotless

text:
NAB buckles down for an 'unwarranted' bank probe.  National Australia Bank chairman Ken Henry is preparing for an unwarranted but necessary banking royal commission, which analysts tip might end a golden era of Australian banking.

The Hayne royal commission into the misconduct of financial institutions is needed to put an end to the political jostling that has plagued the sector, Mr Henry is expected to tell shareholders at the bank's annual general meeting on Friday.

The commission was unwarranted but required to restore trust, respect and confidence, he will say.

NAB chief executive Andrew Thorburn will tell shareholders that the royal co

metadata.headline: NAB buckles down for an 'unwarranted' bank probe

text:
Tokyo Drift.  The global capital of Michelin stars has become the ultimate arena for Australian chefs looking to step up a level

The global capital of Michelin stars has become the ultimate arena for Australian chefs looking to step up a level.   A winter’s night in Healesville, Victoria. With Led Zeppelin on the record player, three friends enjoy shaved truffles atop fresh pasta washed down with a lot of Barolo – and hatch an outlandish plan. They will open a restaurant with only these elements: the pasta, the truffles, the wine, and the Led Zeppelin. “It was a joke for a while,” says Sarah Crago, “but th

End may be near for rate tightening.  Disappointing economic data and a more dovish tone from the Bank of Canada may signal that the end of the global rate tightening cycle is approaching.

The Bank of Canada turned dovish on Wednesday, when it kept its key cash rate on hold at 1.75 per cent, flagging concerns with falling oil prices, slowing domestic growth and risks to global growth on the back of trade disputes. If the mood in Canada is reflected elsewhere, as economists suggest it may, the same could be heard from the Reserve Bank of Australia in 2019.

"I think the more dovish tone you're getting is going to factor into the R

metadata.headline: End may be near for rate tightening

text:
Victorian Liberals face critical vote on future.  The Liberal Party's least-successful division, Victoria, will this weekend choose between a 62-year-old member of the political establishment and a 40-year-old challenger for the party presidency, a position that could prove pivotal in shaping politics in the Labor stronghold.

In a contest that epitomises the chaotic and unpredictable dynamics of the Victorian Liberals, engineer Greg Hannan hopes to defeat interim president and former Victorian attorney-general Robert Clark at a gathering of some 1000 Liberal members at Flemington Racecourse on Saturday.

A grandson of one of prime minister R

metadata.headline: Victorian Liberals face critical vote on future

.  The Australian Financial Review Business Person of the Year, Paul Perreault, will this year spend $US950 million ($1.4 billion) on research and development as part of global blood products group CSL's long-term growth strategy.

Mr Perreault, who was appointed chief executive in July 2013, has put capital investment ahead of dividend payments and share buybacks.

WiseTech boss-backed music platform readies ASX listing.  US-based music licensing platform Songtradr is cranking up the volume on a run at the ASX boards.

Street Talk understands Songtradr and its adviser Blackpeak Capital have been booking in meetings with Australian investors for a pre-IPO funding round, in advance of a run at the local bourse in the second quarter of 2021.

The company - which counts WiseTech chief executive Richard White as its biggest shareholder outside of its founder - was looking to raise $US30 million ($43.6 million), according to a slide deck that's in front of potential investors.

Songtradr is a music licensing platform

metadata.headline: WiseTech boss-backed music platform readies ASX listing
